ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beef Yellow Mee; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific code exists for Filipino beef yellow mee.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate or bowl of beef yellow mee as typically served in a Filipino café in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup / beef noodle dish — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles (NDB 20110), braised beef (NDB 13364), and soy-based broth components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Filipino Beef Yellow Mee exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked egg noodles (~55% of dish weight), braised/stewed beef (~30%), and soy-based gravy/broth (~15%). Weighted composite per 100 g calculated. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on egg noodles GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Filipino Beef Yellow Mee. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on cooked egg noodles GI range of 46–57 reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2008) 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The presence of beef protein and fat further moderates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 61.25) / 100 = 30.6, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 17.5 × 3.5 = 61.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and beef broth, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg/day limit; requesting a reduced-soy-sauce or gravy-on-the-side preparation may help lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~30.6 per serving) from yellow egg noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full serving of beef yellow mee carries a moderate-to-high glycemic load of approximately 30.6 due to the refined yellow egg noodles; choosing a smaller portion, opting for the dry version with less sauce, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation given its saturated fat content (~1.7 g per 100 g, ~6 g per serving) and cholesterol (~38 mg per 100 g) from beef; selecting leaner cuts of beef and limiting portion size aligns with heart-healthy dietary guidelines recommending saturated fat below 7% of total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yellow Mee is a Filipino noodle dish featuring springy yellow egg noodles served with tender sliced or braised beef, typically accompanied by a savoury soy-based gravy or served dry with a light sauce. The dish is commonly found in Filipino cafés and casual dining establishments in Singapore, where it is offered with a choice of gravy (wet) or dry preparation. The yellow noodles are made from wheat flour and egg, giving them a characteristic golden colour, firm bite, and slightly chewy texture. The beef component is usually braised or stewed until tender, absorbing flavours from garlic, onion, soy sauce, and beef broth. Nutritionally, a standard serving (~350 g) provides approximately 518 kcal, 32 g protein, 17 g fat, and 61 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively high in sodium (~1470 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The moderate glycemic load (~30.6) from refined egg noodles warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The beef contributes meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and complete protein, supporting micronutrient adequacy. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef yellow mee?
Is beef yellow mee high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat beef yellow mee?
What is the difference between dry and gravy beef yellow mee?
What noodles are used in beef yellow mee?
Filipino noodle dish with beef Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Filipino Beef Yellow Mee was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked egg noodles and braised beef. GI is estimated from analogous egg noodle data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. The dish is offered in two variants at Lucky Cafe (North Canal Road, Singapore): gravy (wet) and dry; nutrient values represent a weighted average of the two preparations. Halal status is listed as false because beef sourcing and preparation at the specific restaurant have not been verified against halal certification. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' per the input record, consistent with the beef-dominant protein profile of the dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef and egg noodle component data and carry moderate uncertainty.
